name: Naturepedia Geology System Map
description: Directional process map connecting Earth’s internal structure and heat with tectonic motion, plate boundaries, rock formation and transformation, mountain building, faulting, earthquakes, volcanism, geothermal circulation, weathering, erosion, sediment transport, deposition, and landscape evolution.
architecture: Internal Heat & Gravity → Mantle and Lithosphere Behavior → Plate Motion → Boundary Processes → Rock Transformation & Crustal Deformation → Uplift, Volcanism & Faulting → Weathering & Erosion → Sediment Transport & Deposition → Landscape Evolution

systemNodes.name: Earth’s Interior
systemNodes.role: Stores and transfers heat while density, pressure, gravity, and material properties organize Earth’s internal layers.

systemNodes.name: Mantle and Lithosphere
systemNodes.role: Connects slow mantle deformation, rigid lithospheric plates, buoyancy, slab behavior, and plate-driving forces.

systemNodes.name: Plate Motion
systemNodes.role: Moves lithospheric plates relative to one another over geological time.

systemNodes.name: Plate Boundaries
systemNodes.role: Concentrates divergence, convergence, transform motion, deformation, earthquakes, and volcanism.

systemNodes.name: Rock Transformation
systemNodes.role: Connects melting, crystallization, weathering, erosion, deposition, burial, lithification, heat, pressure, fluids, uplift, and exposure.

systemNodes.name: Crustal Deformation
systemNodes.role: Produces folding, faulting, uplift, subsidence, mountain building, and earthquakes.

systemNodes.name: Volcanic and Geothermal Systems
systemNodes.role: Moves magma, heat, fluids, gases, and minerals through volcanic and hydrothermal pathways.

systemNodes.name: Surface Processes
systemNodes.role: Weathering, erosion, mass movement, water, wind, ice, and gravity reshape exposed materials.

systemNodes.name: Sediment System
systemNodes.role: Routes particles and dissolved materials through transport, sorting, deposition, burial, and lithification.

systemNodes.name: Landscape Evolution
systemNodes.role: Expresses the changing balance among uplift, subsidence, rock resistance, weathering, erosion, transport, and deposition.

systemNodes.name: Geological Observation
systemNodes.role: Connects field evidence, samples, maps, seismic data, geodesy, remote sensing, dating, models, and uncertainty assessment.

directedRelationships.relationship: Heat from planetary formation, differentiation, and radioactive decay contributes to internal energy transfer and slow mantle deformation.

directedRelationships.from: mantle behavior, gravity, and plate-boundary forces
directedRelationships.relationship: Slab pull, ridge-related forces, buoyancy, basal interactions, and mantle flow contribute to plate movement.
Extension thins and fractures lithosphere, permitting decompression melting and creation of new oceanic crust in many divergent settings.
A denser oceanic plate may descend beneath another plate, producing trenches, earthquakes, metamorphism, melting, and volcanic arcs.
Compression, folding, thrust faulting, crustal thickening, metamorphism, and uplift can construct mountain belts.
Lateral plate movement concentrates shear stress along faults and can generate earthquakes.
When fault resistance is exceeded, sudden slip releases stored elastic energy as seismic waves and deformation.
Temperature, pressure reduction, water, and material composition influence partial melting in the mantle or crust.
Buoyant magma may rise, stall, crystallize below ground, or erupt as lava, ash, gases, and fragmented material.
Groundwater or seawater can circulate through permeable hot rock, transferring heat and dissolved minerals.
Crystallization produces intrusive or extrusive igneous rocks depending on setting and cooling history.

directedRelationships.from: existing rock exposed at the surface
directedRelationships.relationship: Physical, chemical, and biological weathering break down or alter minerals and rock.
Water, wind, ice, waves, mass movement, and gravity detach and move particles and dissolved material.
Sediment accumulates when carrying capacity declines or when particles settle, precipitate, or become biologically concentrated.
Compaction, cementation, and chemical or biological processes transform deposited sediment into sedimentary rock.

directedRelationships.from: heat, pressure, stress, and reactive fluids
directedRelationships.relationship: Minerals and rock fabrics change without complete melting under new physical and chemical conditions.
Sufficient heating, decompression, volatile addition, or combinations of these processes can partially melt rock.
Uplift and erosion can expose deeply formed rocks to surface weathering.
Crustal uplift and differences in material strength help create and maintain mountains, plateaus, ridges, and escarpments.
Surface processes lower, dissect, redistribute, and rebuild landforms across interacting spatial and temporal scales.

processSequences.name: Divergent Boundary Sequence
sequence: seafloor spreading or continental rift development

processSequences.name: Landscape Sequence
sequence: continued uplift, subsidence, or renewed erosion

feedbackRelationships: uplift increases relief and exposure → erosion can accelerate → unloading can influence crustal response
erosion removes overlying material → deeper rock becomes exposed → weathering begins again
sediment deposition adds load → basin subsidence may continue → additional sediment accommodation develops
fracturing increases permeability → fluid circulation increases → mineral alteration can modify rock strength and permeability
volcanic construction creates relief → weathering and erosion redistribute volcanic material → new soils and sediment systems develop

temporal: days to years of eruption and sediment events
centuries to millennia of landscape adjustment
millions of years of mountain building and basin development
billions of years of planetary evolution

scientificConstraints: Plate motion reflects multiple interacting forces and should not be reduced to one universal driving mechanism.
The rock cycle permits many pathways and does not require every material to pass through one fixed sequence.
Earthquake occurrence is probabilistic; this map does not predict exact earthquake timing or magnitude.
Landscape response depends on climate, water, ice, organisms, material properties, tectonics, gravity, and scale.
Directional relationships represent scientifically grounded process connections without implying identical behavior in every geological setting.
Observation, sampling, dating, modeling, and expert interpretation remain necessary for site-specific conclusions.
